text.content:
Site Overview — The First Under-Road Waystation

Route name: Witness Station Seven. The number is an obligation count, not a sequence — this is one of several Witness Stations on the southern Cairn Hills under-road. The party has no way to know how many others there are.

Construction history

The waystation was built in two phases:

  1. Original mining infrastructure — the first three rooms (the Final Surface Landing, the chamber that became the Account Hall, and what is now the Old Route Office) were cut by Cairn Hills miners working a now-exhausted seam of black mineral roughly four to five centuries ago. The miners did not know they were cutting into an existing system; the system found them.
  2. Conversion to a witness station — sometime in the mid-4th to early 5th century CY, the rooms were expanded, the tally walls cut, the cistern slit deepened, the Black Ledger Gate installed at the lower end. The mason work of the conversion is older-style and excellent. It has aged into the rock as though the rock invited it.

Layout

The waystation is a single level, roughly L-shaped, with the cistern slit running along the long side. From entry (the Final Surface Landing) to exit (the Lower Route Fork beyond the Black Ledger Gate), it is approximately 240 feet across the long axis and 90 feet across the short axis. Compact. The party can walk the whole site in fifteen minutes if they hurry.

Staffing (when active)

Full active staffing is:

  • 1 Route Warden (Savax, currently).
  • 3 to 5 Route-Cleaners.
  • 1 to 2 Silent Couriers in transit at any given time.
  • 1 Under-Road Scout monitoring the upper stair.
  • 0 to 2 witnesses being held or transferred through.

Total: 6–11 personnel when active. The waystation can be operated by as few as 2 (Savax plus one cleaner) when in emergency-closure mode.

Lighting

Lamps in protected wall niches, fuelled by a mineral oil pressed from the same seams the original miners worked. The oil burns hot, smokeless, and yellow. The lamps are dim by surface standards — about half the brightness of a Greyhawk City street lamp. Vision past 30 feet is at disadvantage even with all lamps lit.

If lamps are extinguished (route-cleaners do this during emergency sweeps), the chambers fall to true darkness. Most route personnel have darkvision (60 ft.) or carry small shielded lamps clipped at the belt.

Air

Air at the waystation is breathable but thin — closer to mountain-pass air than surface air. PCs feel the difference within an hour. The cistern slit and certain vents move air through, but slowly. See the Breath and Stone Clock for what happens when air movement is disrupted.

Sound

Sound carries strangely. Footsteps muffle near the tally walls; speech echoes in the Account Hall; the cistern slit conducts whispers along its length for surprising distances. The waystation is designed for sound control — the wardens know how to use signal tones (the seven-count bell) without their tones carrying past the Black Ledger Gate.

Smell

Cold stone, lamp oil, and faintly — not unpleasantly — a smell like dry paper warmed in sun. The party may not be able to place the smell at first. It is the smell of the witness registry's wax seals, which have been re-warmed and re-pressed enough times that the wax has thinned to a paper-like translucency.

Map layout (text description)

For reference; see also the Map Briefs at the end of this journal. Locations 1–12 chain together as follows:

  • Location 1: Final Surface Landing — entry from above.
  • Short corridor leads to:
  • Location 2: Account Hall — the main chamber. Branches off to:
  • Location 3: Witness Registry Wall (along the long left wall of the Account Hall — not a separate room).
  • Location 4: Toll-Token Cage (an iron-grated alcove off the Account Hall's south side).
  • Location 5: Old Route Office (off the north side of the Account Hall).
  • Location 6: Sleeping Alcoves (off the south corridor, six small niches).
  • Location 7: Supply Cache (locked room off the south corridor, beyond the alcoves).
  • Location 8: Burn Furnace (small chamber off the north corridor).
  • Location 9: Sealed Witness Cell (off the north corridor, beyond the Burn Furnace).
  • Location 10: Cistern Slit (runs along the entire long side of the waystation, with multiple access points).
  • Location 11: Under-Road Marker Gallery (corridor from the back of the Account Hall to the Warden Post).
  • Location 12: Warden Post (small chamber with overview slit into the Account Hall and the only direct access to the Black Ledger Gate).

The Black Ledger Gate (Location 13) and the Lower Route Fork (Location 14) are documented in their own dedicated journal ("Phase 9 — The Black Ledger Gate & Lower Route Fork").

Location 1 — Final Surface Landing

The stair from Tarnsmere ends at a long landing roughly twelve feet wide and twenty feet long. This is where surface masonry ends and the under-road begins. Both are visible in the same view: the upper end of the landing is dressed civic stone (mid-5th century Aerdy Permissive Survey work), and the lower end is the older, deeper-cut, black-seamed stone of the waystation proper.

What the party sees

The party arrives here at the end of the Phase 9 opening read-aloud. Add the following details on inspection:

The last surface-facing inscription

At the upper end of the landing, cut into the lintel of the stair archway, in early Aerdy commercial script:

Accounts above. Witness below.

The phrase is the same as the Phase 8 Permission Door's lintel, but here it has been re-cut once more — the cuts are deeper, the surface scrubbed clean of dust. Someone has tended this inscription within the last month.

Tally marks begin

At the lower end of the landing, the wall on the left-hand side bears its first tally row: six vertical cuts in three pairs, each pair crossed by a small horizontal at the base. Below the row, a single circular impression — a token socket. The socket is empty.

The draft

The air at the landing does something unusual. It moves sideways, not down. A lit lamp held at chest height shows the smoke drifting at a slow steady angle toward the left-hand wall, where the cistern slit runs underneath. The draft is barely perceptible without a flame.

Scuff marks and traces

The landing floor has been recently cleaned, but not perfectly. Investigation DC 13 reveals:

  • A narrow, careful boot scuff at the third step from the bottom of the surface stair — the same boot style as the Phase 8 route-cleaner traces.
  • If Kethren passed through here in the last month: a single grey-and-bone thread caught in the masonry seam at the landing's lower end.
  • If Haskur passed through here in the last month: a faint smear of expensive Greyhawk-style boot polish at the landing's upper end, on a corner where a satchel might have brushed. Investigation DC 15 to identify the polish as the kind sold from a single specific Garden Quarter cobbler — the same one Haskur used in Phase 6.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation13Identifies the masonry-phase shift; finds the cleaner boot scuff and Kethren thread.
Investigation15Identifies Haskur's boot polish if applicable.
Survival14Tracks the most recent passage; estimates 2–5 people moved down this landing in the last two weeks.
History15Recognises that the surface-facing toll language ("Accounts", "Witness") has been replaced below by route language (the tally marks). Knows this is the bureaucratic boundary.
Arcana16Detects faint residual route-warding bound to the empty token socket. The warding is not active — it would activate if a wrong token were forced into the socket.

What the token socket does

If a Tarnsmere descent token is placed in the empty socket, nothing visible happens at the landing — but a single soft tone sounds in the Account Hall ahead (Location 2). This is a standard entry confirmation; it identifies the party (or whoever placed the token) as an authorized descent arrival. Wardens hearing it will treat the arrival as expected.

If the party did not bring a token forward from Phase 8: the socket can be left empty without consequence. The party arrives unannounced, which is sometimes useful and sometimes complicated — see Opening branches.

Carry-forward to Phase 10

This is the last surface-facing inscription on the route. PCs who read it carefully here will recognise its absence below as part of the procedural shift — below this landing, surface language stops being used.

Location 2 — Account Hall

The largest chamber in the waystation. Roughly 50 feet long by 35 feet wide, ceiling 12 feet at its peak. Low light from three lamps in deep wall niches.

What the party sees

The hall is built like a public records office that has been carved out of the wrong colour stone. Seven long counter-bays of dark dressed stone run along the long walls — three on the south side, four on the north. Each bay has a stone counter at standing height, a token slot set into the counter's centre, and a column of tally marks cut into the wall directly above. There are no chairs. There are no inkwells. There are no paper ledgers. There is nothing soft anywhere in this room.

The floor between the counter rows is broad and unobstructed. Drainage channels run along the long edges, leading toward a slit in the south wall that opens onto the cistern. Old lamp brackets stand at the corners, with reservoirs of yellow oil and bowls of mineral wicks beside them.

The walls, where they are not counter-bays, are tally walls. The long left-hand wall as you enter has the densest concentration — the Witness Registry Wall, which is Location 3 in this journal but is physically part of this chamber.

Black mineral seams run through the stone in long horizontal threads, catching the lamplight in a way that almost suggests writing — until you look directly at them, when they resolve back into geology.

The seven counter-bays

Each bay handles a different kind of transfer. From left to right, on the south wall:

  1. Bay 1: Surface arrivals. Token slot is sized for a Tarnsmere descent token. This is where the party would be received in a normal descent.
  2. Bay 2: Witness intake. Token slot is sized for a smaller, square witness token. The slot is currently empty. A scratched-out wax plate is attached to the counter with a chip of black wax.
  3. Bay 3: Recovery intake. Token slot is sized for a larger, asymmetric token marked with a notch. This is the bay through which Haskur was processed as a recovered asset, if his recovery happened.

On the north wall, left to right:

  1. Bay 4: Cargo passage. No token slot. A simple ledger groove and a tally tool rack.
  2. Bay 5: Courier transit. Token slot for a route courier token — Kethren's mark, or one like it, fits here.
  3. Bay 6: Escort declaration. Two adjacent token slots — for escort and escorted simultaneously.
  4. Bay 7: Supply receipt. Token slot for crate-tally tokens. This is where the Blooming Rot crates would have been logged.

Skill checks — what the party can extract

CheckDCYields
Investigation14Finds the H.V.A. transfer line at Bay 3. The line reads (in route shorthand): “Recovered cargo. Escort: K. Witness pending. Recoverable but unreliable. Surface name attached: Andren.”
Insight15Realises the hall is designed to process people as documents. The token slots are not for keys; they are for declarations. What you put in the slot is what you are.
Arcana15Detects old non-spell warding logic bound to the token slots. The warding is not magical in any active sense — it is more like the way an old lock has been used so often that its tumblers move only in the patterns the keys have taught them. The slots reject malformed tokens by feel, not by spell.
History16Identifies the script convention as a stripped-down, almost glyphic form of Aerdy commercial-court shorthand — but cleaner, with no concessions to surface readers. This is what Aerdy commercial-court shorthand looked like in the early 4th century, before surface scribes added padding for legibility. The route uses the older form.

Evidence available here

  • Haskur's H.V.A. transfer line (if not yet destroyed by the Record Destruction Clock).
  • Kethren's courier mark attached at Bay 5 as the current authorized escort.
  • Recent activity at Bay 7 — crate-tally marks consistent with Blooming Rot sample movement.
  • A blank witness-bay declaration at Bay 2 — the slot is awaiting a token. If the party places a witness token here later, they declare themselves as witnesses. This is one path through the Black Ledger Gate's False Category Method.

If the party is here during the Correction encounter

See "Phase 9 — Clocks & Pressure” for the Correction at the Account Hall encounter, triggered by Alert Clock 4 or by other escalations. Terrain features: stone counters (half-cover), token slots (interaction objects), low light (disadvantage on Perception past 30 ft.), burn oil reservoirs (fire hazard if shot or shattered), hanging tally plates (can be pulled down for cover or to obscure a registry column being scraped), shutter slits (allow the warden in Location 12 to see and signal), side ducts to the cistern slit.

Location 3 — Witness Registry Wall

The long left-hand wall of the Account Hall, considered as its own location for evidence purposes. About 50 feet long, 9 feet high to the lamp niches. Densely worked stone, cut with shallow vertical columns. Each column is a witness register.

What the party sees

The wall is divided into shallow vertical columns by lightly raised stone ridges, like a sheet of music ruled for a system you do not know. Each column is about a hand's breadth wide. Each column is filled, top to bottom, with marks.

Some marks are simple: a single horizontal cut, a single small notch, a circle. Others are layered — two cuts at one height, then a notch above, then a small cross above that. The pattern repeats with variations down the column.

Some columns are old. The cuts have softened and the chip-edges have rounded over. Some columns are recent. A few cuts have fresh chips still in the dust at the floor below.

One column, about a third of the way along the wall, has a small wax plate stuck to it with a chip of black wax. The wax plate bears a single word in surface script:

Andren

The wax is fresh. The plate is, very obviously, a temporary attachment. Someone intends to remove it.

How the marks read

The wall does not record names. It records transfers as combinations of:

  • Status mark (cargo, witness, escort, escortee, recovered).
  • Direction mark (descent, ascent, lateral transfer, deferral).
  • Authority mark (which warden or route system authorized).
  • Outcome mark (completed, witnessed, recoverable, lost, substituted).

A typical transfer entry is 4–7 cuts arranged in a small block, occupying about an inch and a half of column height.

What the party can recover

Haskur's entry (if not yet scraped)

The wax plate marked “Andren” is attached to a fresh registry entry. The entry reads (Investigation DC 15, History DC 16, or Arcana DC 16 to translate):

Status: recovered cargo.
Direction: descent (continued).
Authority: [Kethren's courier mark — the thin vertical stroke crossed at three-quarter height].
Outcome: witnessed, recoverable, conditional unreliable.

The “conditional unreliable” outcome is rare. Most outcomes are simple. This one is unusual and tells the party that whoever Haskur is, the route does not entirely trust him — and does not entirely trust whoever placed him in this registry. The wardens are uneasy about Haskur.

Kethren's authority mark

Kethren's mark appears at the top of several recent entries — not just Haskur's. The Witness Registry Wall shows Kethren as an escort authority for at least seven transfers in the last two months. He is a frequent traveler.

Investigation DC 17 reads further: most of Kethren's entries are downward transfers (descent). One is an ascent transfer — he came up recently, then went back down. The ascent corresponds in date to the Phase 8 site activity above. Kethren came up to manage something on the surface, and went back.

The unknown authority mark

One mark appears above several of the most important recent entries — a mark the party does not recognise from any surface system. It is a single curved horizontal cut, like a shallow arc, with three small notches at its base.

This mark appears above Kethren's mark on the entries it touches. It is the mark of a deeper authority. It has authorized at least four transfers in the last six weeks. The party cannot identify who or what this authority is. They can record the mark.

(GM note: this is the “E.” mark in its route-system form. Do not say so. Do not confirm. Let the party photograph it / sketch it / rub it / take a wax impression and carry it back.)

Handout

The party recovers Handout 2 — Witness Tally Fragment here. See the Handouts journal. Recovery options:

  • Investigation DC 15 — reads the column. The party records what they read.
  • Thieves' tools or stonecunning — lifts the wax plate cleanly. The plate is small enough to carry. Lifting it does not destroy the registry entry beneath.
  • Mason's tools DC 16 — carefully removes a small block of the wall containing the H.V.A. block. Slow, loud, advances Record Destruction Clock.
  • Wax impression — the party can make a wax impression of the column with a torch and a candle. Investigation DC 13. This is the best non-destructive option.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation15Reads the pattern at the column level.
History16Compares marks to old surface toll and guild tally systems. Confirms the route system is a precursor of the Aerdy commercial-court tally — or, more precisely, that the Aerdy tally is a simplified surface descendant of this older system.
Arcana16Identifies the residual warding on the wall as procedural rather than magical. The wall does not enforce anything. It records.
Religion17Recognises the registry's authority structure as analogous to certain very old Suel or pre-Suel temple-record conventions — but not derived from them. The route system is not religious. It uses some of the same syntax.

Location 4 — Toll-Token Cage

A small alcove off the south side of the Account Hall, sealed by an iron grating about 10 feet wide and 8 feet tall. The grating has a simple but well-made lock (Thieves' Tools DC 14).

What's inside

The cage is roughly 8 feet by 10 feet, fitted with stone shelving on three walls. The shelves hold tokens: round, square, notched, and split discs of brass-and-tin alloy, lead, and a few of polished black stone. Each token has a function in the route's procedural language.

Most shelves are mostly empty. The tokens that remain are organized by category — surface arrivals, witnesses, escorts, recovery, supply, courier, escort-pair, gate-pair. There are also a few shelves marked simply blank, holding undifferentiated discs of the right size and weight to be cast into any category.

Items of interest

  • Surface arrival tokens (3 remaining) — round, the same shape as Tarnsmere descent tokens. If the party has none, taking one of these is useful.
  • Witness tokens (5 remaining) — small, square, with a single notch on one edge. Used at Account Hall Bay 2.
  • Escort tokens (2 remaining) — thin, narrow, with three notches in a row.
  • One damaged lower-route token — cracked but repairable. With Smith's or Tinker's Tools DC 15 and 10 minutes, a PC can mend it. This token is the key to forging a Black Ledger Gate Forged Tally Method opening (see the Gate journal).
  • One counterfeit token — planted by route-cleaners during a previous sweep, deliberately. Sized correctly but with subtle wrong marks. Investigation DC 14 identifies it; Arcana DC 16 confirms it. Using the counterfeit at the Black Ledger Gate triggers the gate's Backlash hazard.
  • One blank token — an undifferentiated brass-and-tin disc. With Smith's tools DC 17 and the right reference (the Account Hall's token slot patterns), a clever PC can cast a useful token from this blank. Slow: 1–2 hours.
  • Route-token molds — three small bronze molds, each for a different token category. Useful only with the blank, and only if the party has the patience and the tools.

What the missing tokens tell the party

Investigation DC 13 inventories the shelves. The party can infer which tokens have been removed recently:

  • Two recovery tokens removed within the last month. (One was used for Haskur. The other was used for an unidentified recovery the party will hear about later.)
  • Three witness tokens removed within the last six weeks. (Used for the Unclaimed Witness and two others.)
  • One deep-route courier token removed within the last week. (Used by Kethren on his last ascent and re-descent.)
  • One gate-pair token removed within the last three days. (Used by an unnamed deeper figure who passed through — the party has no further information about this.)

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Thieves' Tools14Picks the cage lock.
Investigation13Inventories what's been recently removed.
Investigation14Identifies the counterfeit token.
Arcana16Confirms tokens are mundane objects carrying procedural authority, not magic keys.
Smith's / Tinker's Tools15Repairs the damaged lower-route token.
Smith's / Tinker's Tools17Casts a new token from a blank using one of the molds. Takes 1–2 hours and is loud (advances Alert Clock).

What the party should leave with

At minimum: one surface arrival token (if they don't already have one), one witness token, and the damaged lower-route token. These three together open multiple methods at the Black Ledger Gate. The counterfeit should be left or marked clearly to avoid accidental use.

Location 5 — Old Route Office

A small, cramped office off the north side of the Account Hall. About 12 feet by 14 feet. This is where surface paperwork is converted into route language before being filed or destroyed.

What the party sees

The room is the smallest you have entered. It is also the only one with shelves — not shelves of stone, but shelves of black wood polished to a near-mirror finish, fitted into the walls without visible joinery. The shelves carry stone tablets, wax plates, and short rolls of heat-scarred parchment. There is no paper here that has not been moved down from above.

A low writing desk runs along the south wall. A small heating brazier with an iron lid sits beside the desk. A row of mineral-ink wells — six of them, marked in different colours of stone-paste — sits beside the brazier. There is no chair. The clerk who works here works standing.

On the desk, a half-finished conversion: a surface packet, unfolded; beside it, a stone tablet bearing the route summary of its contents. The packet is from V. Korre's office. The summary is in route shorthand. The packet is being converted right now, or was being converted when the office's last occupant left.

What's recoverable

The half-finished conversion (Handout 3)

The packet on the desk is a Korre channel directive, summarised on the adjacent stone tablet. Both can be read by the party.

The packet says, in clear surface script: “Andren cleared for recovery; escort assigned (K.); deliver below; surface name to be filed in dead-name registry. Korre signs and seals. Channel closes after this dispatch.”

The summary tablet says, in route shorthand: “Surface names converted. Korre channel closed. Andren unreliable. Escort carries second page. Witness below only after tally.”

The two texts together tell the party several things:

  • Korre's surface authority has formally ended at the waystation. Her last dispatch was an authorization for Haskur's recovery.
  • “Andren unreliable” is a route-side annotation — added by the clerk doing the conversion, not by Korre. The waystation does not trust Haskur even though Korre cleared him.
  • The phrase “escort carries second page” means Kethren is carrying a second part of the dispatch that did not stay at this station. It went deeper with him.

The colour-coded inks

The six mineral-ink wells (black, white-grey, blue-black, rust-red, ochre, deep green) are used to colour-code conversions by category: routine, urgent, exceptional, terminating, recoverable, escort-required. The current state of the wells — which inks are recently used — tells the party that two terminating conversions were done in the last week. The party can deduce that two surface identities have been formally killed in route language recently. (One is Haskur's “Andren”. The other is unknown.)

The brazier

The heating brazier in this office is small — not for destruction; for activating heat-reactive inks on incoming surface packets. The party finds, in the brazier's residue, scraps of two heat-activated phrases that have already been read and converted:

  • “...recoverable but unreliable; surface name attached.”
  • “...witness below after tally.”

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation13Recovers the Korre channel closure on the desk.
History15Interprets the route-shorthand summary tablet.
Arcana15Detects heat-reactive writing residue in the brazier — matches the same residue from the Phase 6 scroll tubes.
Investigation16Identifies which mineral inks have been used most recently (the two terminating conversions).
Insight14Notes that the conversion was abandoned mid-task. The clerk left in a hurry, or under orders, after starting this work.

Handout

The party recovers Handout 3 — Conversion Note here. Reproduce both the surface packet and the route summary tablet. Their juxtaposition is the point: same content, two languages, two procedures, one obligation.

Location 6 — Sleeping Alcoves

Six narrow niches set into the south corridor wall. Each niche is roughly 7 feet deep, 4 feet wide, and 5 feet tall — you cannot stand upright inside. Each contains a stone bunk, a grey wool blanket folded with mechanical precision, and a small sealed personal hole at the head of the bunk. No mirrors anywhere.

What the party sees

The corridor runs perhaps thirty feet, with six niches on the left and a long blank wall of dressed stone on the right. The lamps here are dimmer than in the hall — one lamp per pair of niches, set into the ceiling between them.

The niches are obviously functional. They are not obviously slept in. The bunks are clean, the blankets are folded, the personal holes are sealed with wax. There is no detritus — no dropped buttons, no spilt water, no hair or skin oil on the stone. Either no one has slept here in weeks, or the people who sleep here clean meticulously each time they wake.

What the party can find

Recent use traces (Perception DC 13)

One niche (the third from the entrance, by count) shows signs of recent occupation. The blanket is folded slightly less precisely than the others. The wax on the personal hole is fresher. The stone of the bunk has a faintly warmer patch — warm by minutes, not hours. Someone was sleeping here within the last forty minutes.

The personal hole, opened (Thieves' Tools DC 12 or just prying the wax with a blade), contains a small leather pouch with: a stone-paste ink stylus, a single witness token, a folded scrap of paper bearing four hand-drawn route marks (a study key for new cleaners learning the system), and a small bone fingerstamp identical in mark to Kethren's courier signature but cruder — a junior version.

The panic prayer (Perception DC 13)

The fifth niche, scratched into the stone of the inner wall, behind where a blanket would normally lie:

Saint Cuthbert preserve me whole. Do not let them recall me below the third gate. I have not asked to be witnessed. I have done what I was told and I did not ask. Do not let them recall me.

The scratching is fresh — within the last six months. The hand is a surface hand, not a route hand. Someone who was nominally part of the route's surface arm wrote this in panic.

The third gate

The scratched prayer's reference to “the third gate” is a Phase 9 hook into Phase 10 and beyond. The Black Ledger Gate is the first gate; there are more below. The party should not learn how many. Religion or Insight DC 14 interprets the prayer correctly as a plea not to be moved further down the route.

Kethren's bedding (or absence thereof)

If Kethren passed through here recently: none of the alcoves were used by him. He does not sleep on the route — or, more accurately, he does not sleep this way. The party will not find his trace in the alcoves.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Perception13Finds the recent-use niche and the scratched prayer.
Thieves' Tools12Opens the sealed personal hole.
Religion / Insight14Interprets the panic prayer; identifies “the third gate” reference.
Investigation14Identifies the bone fingerstamp as a junior version of Kethren's courier mark.

Optional carry-forward

If the party identifies the panic prayer's author through follow-up investigation in Phase 10 (a missing-persons follow-up via Iren or Caelith), they may locate the person. They are either dead, recalled, or hiding. This is a Phase 10 hook for the GM to seed here.

Location 7 — Supply Cache

A locked room off the south corridor, beyond the alcoves. Approximately 15 feet by 20 feet. Heavy iron door with a route-warden lock (Thieves' Tools DC 16, or Force DC 18 if the party is willing to make noise).

What's inside

Crates from both surface and deeper-route fronts. The cache is mixed: some crates are surface-origin (Cindren & Vhal stencils, Kestrel & Reed seals, generic Hardby grain-merchant marks), some are deeper-origin (route-shorthand tally tags, no surface markings, sealed in the same wax as the Account Hall's witness plates).

Items in the cache

  • Grain sacks (4) — surface-marked, mostly Hardby. The grain is good. Crucially, two of the sacks contain blackened seed samples — identifiable on Nature DC 14 as Blooming Rot affected seed at an early but unmistakable stage. These are samples, not bulk supply: the route was moving them through, not eating them.
  • Treated wax packets (6) — small wax-sealed packets containing dried plant material (Nature DC 15 to identify as Blooming Rot stalk fibre at different stages of decay). The packets are labelled in route shorthand with date markers and source markers. These are the operation's reference samples for studying decay progression.
  • Medical wraps and supplies (1 small crate) — bandages, bonesetter's splints, vials of clean water in glass, two doses of common healing-herb tincture. Medicine DC 14 identifies this as supplies prepared for extracted agents — people retrieved from surface operations, possibly injured during retrieval. Consistent with what Haskur would have needed if his Phase 6 contingency-escape left him hurt.
  • Travel cloaks (8) — identical, undyed wool, route-cleaner cut. Spare uniforms.
  • Sealed food (a few small crates) — not from the surface. Dense, oily, vacuum-tight in wax. Nature DC 15: this is travel food, prepared from materials the party cannot identify with surface knowledge. (It is, in fact, route-prepared from sub-surface lichen and pressed insect protein. The party should not be told this. They can recognise that it is not surface-sourced.)
  • Ledgers converted to stone-tally tags (12) — small stone tags with route marks, used as inventory references. Each tag corresponds to a surface ledger entry that has been formally converted. Reading the tags (Investigation DC 14, History DC 15) shows that surface supply ledgers from at least three Hardby and Greyhawk City suppliers have been read, converted, and filed below. The route knows about the party's surface allies' supply chains.

Crate tags — the Recovery marker

Several crates bear an unusual tag: a small stone disc reading “recovery” rather than “shipment”. Investigation DC 15 connects these tags to:

  • The Blooming Rot sample movement — samples were collected after apparent crop failure on specific Hardby and Yeomanry farms, then moved below for analysis.
  • Bonded claim filings — the tags reference Cindren & Vhal claim numbers from Phases 2–4. Cross-referencing: the claims were filed in the same week as the crops failed, in some cases the same day, in two cases the day before.

The pattern matters: the recovery infrastructure existed before the damage occurred. The party can extrapolate: this was not a disaster the conspiracy reacted to. It was a damage event the conspiracy planned around.

Handout

The party recovers Handout 4 — Recovery Crate Tag here. See the Handouts journal.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Thieves' Tools16Picks the lock.
Nature14Identifies treated seed samples as Blooming Rot affected.
Nature15Identifies the treated wax packets as decay-progression study material; identifies the sealed food as non-surface origin.
Investigation15Connects crate marks to Blooming Rot evidence chain.
Investigation17Cross-references stone-tally tags to specific Cindren & Vhal claim numbers; confirms claim-filing predates damage.
Medicine14Identifies the medical supplies as prepared for extracted/injured agents.
Arcana16Detects concealment marks on the seed packets — minor warding to keep the samples viable longer than surface storage would.

What the party should leave with

At minimum: one Recovery Crate Tag, one wax packet of decay-progression sample material (sealed; do not open unless they have a Nature expert and a clean lab in Phase 10), and at least one of the stone-tally tags referencing a Cindren & Vhal claim. These three together establish the Recovery dimension of the conspiracy's evidence pattern.

Location 8 — Burn Furnace

A small, hot chamber off the north corridor. About 10 feet by 12 feet. The chamber's south wall is dominated by a controlled stone furnace, vented up a narrow chimney shaft that disappears into the ceiling.

Purpose

This is where the waystation destroys surface paper, clothing tags, temporary surface-side names, and any other material that cannot be filed in route language. The furnace is hot enough to reduce parchment, wax, leather, and most fabrics to ash within minutes.

What the party sees

The chamber is hot. The furnace's iron grate is open, fed from the front by a small loading hopper. A wrought-iron tongs leans against the wall. A pair of ash drawers slides out from below the grate; the upper drawer is partly full of pale grey ash, the lower drawer empty and warm.

On the floor beside the furnace, a small basket of items waiting to be burned: a stack of folded surface-script slips, a wax-sealed packet, a pair of leather gloves, and what appears to be the corner of a uniform tabard with an embroidered seal that has been cut away.

The lamps in this room are extra-bright. Burning work is precise work.

What's recoverable (if the Record Destruction Clock is 2 or lower)

The H.V.A. surface-name slip (Furnace Scrap, Handout 5)

Among the slips in the basket: a folded paper with the name “Andren” in surface script on one side, and on the other side, in a different ink — not Korre's hand, not Haskur's hand — a single line:

Blooming was proof. Rot was proof. Recovery was the question.

The slip is intact. Recovery DC: Investigation DC 14 to grab it without scattering the basket; Dexterity save DC 13 if the party member is careless with the hot ash drawers.

The line itself is the Phase 9 thematic centerpiece. Whoever wrote it on the same slip as the surface name was a route clerk who was perhaps not entirely on the program — the line is too philosophical for normal route administration. Insight DC 16 confirms: this is a clerk's editorial gloss, written in a quiet moment, on a slip that was about to be destroyed. The clerk knew it would never be read by an outsider. They were wrong.

Other recoverable items

  • The wax-sealed packet contains two more surface-name slips, names the party may recognise from Phase 6 or Phase 7 (GM choice; suggest: one Phase 6 minor courier, one Phase 7 false-stair survivor). Both marked “recovered, terminating.”
  • The uniform tabard corner, with its embroidered seal cut away, was the seal of a minor Aerdy commercial-court customs office (Investigation DC 15) — an office the party has not encountered yet, but which is on the Phase 10 trail.

Hazards

  • Backdraft if the furnace door is opened wide while feeding it — Dexterity save DC 14 or take 2d6 fire damage. Investigation DC 13 predicts the risk.
  • Hot ash in the drawers. Careless reaching: Dexterity save DC 13 or take 1d4 fire damage and lose any paper recovery from that drawer.
  • Smoke if the chimney is blocked or damaged. Confined-space; advances the Breath and Stone Clock by 1 if combat occurs in this chamber.

Enemy priority

Route-cleaners prioritize this room above all others. When the Alert Clock rises, expect cleaners to converge on the Burn Furnace and begin feeding the basket into the fire. Once items reach the grate, they are gone in under a minute. The party can interrupt them with combat, with stealth, with a thrown lamp, with anything — but they need to act, not deliberate.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation14Recovers H.V.A. slip without damage.
Arcana15Identifies heat-reactive ink traces — the same chemistry as the Phase 6 scroll tubes and the Old Route Office brazier.
Dexterity save13Avoids hot-ash injury when handling drawers carelessly.
Dexterity save14Avoids backdraft if furnace is opened wide during active feeding.
Insight16Identifies the “Blooming was proof” line as a route clerk's editorial gloss.
Investigation15Identifies the cut-away tabard seal as a minor Aerdy customs-office insignia.

Handout

The party recovers Handout 5 — Furnace Scrap here. This is the most important single piece of paper in Phase 9.

Location 9 — Sealed Witness Cell

A small holding chamber at the end of the north corridor. About 8 feet by 10 feet. The cell has no bed. It has a stone bench along one wall, a water slot in another wall, a token ring at the door, and a single route mark above the lintel: the holding-for-witness mark, which translates roughly as “awaiting transfer authorization.”

What this chamber is

Not a prison in surface terms. This is a place to hold a person whose transfer is in process but not yet authorised. The route does not consider this incarceration — it considers it documentation pending. People held here are not punished. They are catalogued.

What the party sees

The cell door stands ajar by a hand's breadth. The token ring at the lintel is empty — whoever last held it has taken the token with them. The cell beyond is dim and small.

Inside: a stone bench, a water slot (a narrow horizontal opening through which water can be passed in or out from the outside), and a single old blood stain on the floor near the bench. The stain is dark, almost black with age — weeks old, perhaps a month.

And, sitting on the bench, neither standing nor moving, is a person.

The occupant — possibilities

The GM should choose one occupant based on play state, or roll if uncertain:

1. The Unclaimed Witness (Merrit Osk) — default

A former surface courier, taken below as a witness three weeks ago. Traumatized but precise. See the NPC journal for full details. This is the most useful and most interesting occupant for the party.

2. An abandoned surface courier

A genuinely confused surface employee of one of the Phase 6 fronts (Brass Crow, Kestrel & Reed). They were brought here as a witness, held while their fate was decided, and then forgotten by the route's general administrative process. Less useful than Merrit Osk but a good moral complication if the GM wants a less-articulate captive.

3. A terrified route-cleaner

One of the route's own people, demoted to witness status as discipline for a recent failure. Knows route procedure but cannot or will not betray the route. Useful for the party only if pressured.

4. A dead witness

The cell's occupant is already dead — killed in the last 24 hours, possibly by route-cleaners disposing of an inconvenient witness. Medicine DC 14 confirms recent death; Investigation DC 15 identifies the killer's mark on the body's wrist (the route's “substituted witness” mark).

5. Empty but recently used

No one is here. Investigation DC 13 confirms recent use; the water slot was passed water within the day, the bench is still slightly warm, the blood stain is older than the recent use. The witness has been moved on — through the Black Ledger Gate, presumably.

6. Haskur Vandrell

Only if Haskur is alive but captured by route forces between Phase 8 and Phase 9. He is held here pending transfer. He is afraid. He is willing to talk. See his NPC entry for guidance. This option is the most dramatic but should be used carefully — Haskur in custody as a witness reframes the entire phase.

7. A replacement witness

If Haskur is dead, the route's procedure requires a substituted witness — someone else to attest to the transfer that Haskur was supposed to witness. The substitute is a frightened surface clerk or junior cleaner. They do not know what they are witnessing.

The blood stain

Regardless of occupant: the blood stain on the floor is an artifact of an earlier occupant. Medicine DC 14 dates it as roughly one month old. The blood is from a single individual, no fight, no struggle — consistent with a quiet execution. The route does this occasionally.

The water slot

The slot allows water to be passed in and out without opening the cell door. It is also wide enough to pass small objects through, with effort. A clever PC could use it to slip a witness token to an occupant from outside — useful for getting an occupant to declare themselves witnessed for the Black Ledger Gate.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation13Finds recent use signs; identifies the cell's last use.
Medicine14Dates the blood stain and (if applicable) the occupant's condition.
Insight15Reading an occupant who is alive: confirms they fear recall more than death.
Persuasion13–17Varies by occupant. Merrit Osk talks at DC 13; a route-cleaner at DC 17.

The phrase “fear of recall”

Any occupant who can speak will, eventually, mention “recall” with audible fear. The route does not punish in surface terms. It recalls — meaning, it moves the witness further down the route to a place from which return is not procedurally available. To be recalled is to become permanent cargo. This is what occupants in this cell are most afraid of.

Location 10 — Cistern Slit

A narrow water channel running along the entire south edge of the waystation, from the Final Surface Landing's drift-air vent to the cistern outflow well past the Black Ledger Gate. Not a natural stream. Part of ventilation, waste, sound control, and (occasionally) bypass.

Description

The slit is roughly two feet wide at floor level and four feet tall. The water in it is dark — black, in the lamplight, though not opaque; you can see your hand below the surface if you put it in. The slit's walls are smooth stone, slick with mineral residue. The water moves slowly, perhaps a foot a second, from west to east.

Maintenance bridges of dark iron cross the slit at three points: at the Account Hall's south side, between the Sleeping Alcoves and the Supply Cache, and at the back of the chamber leading to the Warden Post. Each bridge is narrow — barely a foot wide — with no railing.

Side ducts branch from the slit at irregular intervals, carrying air and sound between chambers.

Uses for the party

Bypass

The slit can be entered and crawled or swum — difficult, but possible. From the Supply Cache or the Sleeping Alcoves, a small party can drop into the slit, traverse along the south edge of the waystation underwater or in the air pocket above the water, and emerge at any of the bridge access points, or continue past the Warden Post and the Black Ledger Gate's base.

Specifically, the slit allows a Black Ledger Gate Bypass Method (Method F): one or two PCs can crawl through the slit, around the gate, and emerge in the antechamber on the far side. They can then open the gate from the inside.

Ambush

The slit's bridges and side ducts make excellent ambush positions. The party can drop a route-cleaner pursuer into the slit from a bridge (Strength contest, DC 14 to push). The cleaner will not drown easily — the slit is shallow at most points — but they will be wet, cold, and momentarily out of the encounter.

Hidden listening

The side ducts conduct sound. Perception DC 15 from any duct opening lets the party hear route signals (the seven-count bell), wardens conferring, route-cleaners coordinating, or the occupant of the Witness Cell if they are speaking aloud.

Environmental cover

The slit's continuous draft is the only consistent moving air in the waystation. If the Breath and Stone Clock advances and the air thins, retreating to within ten feet of the slit's water surface gives a Constitution save advantage against the air-thinness effect.

Hazards

Sudden water pulse

If a Breath and Stone Clock segment advances to 3 (Cistern Pressure Rises), the water surges. Anyone in the slit at that moment makes a Dexterity save DC 14 or be knocked prone and pushed 10 feet downstream. Anyone on a bridge above makes a Dexterity save DC 12 or be knocked prone.

Cold and grip

Long swims in the slit cause Constitution checks (DC 13 every minute) or take 1 level of exhaustion from cold and exertion. The mineral residue on the walls makes climbing back out of the slit at a non-bridge point an Athletics DC 15 check.

Air pockets

The slit has air above the water at most points, but in two narrow sections (one near the Account Hall, one near the Warden Post), the ceiling dips and only the water flows through. A PC must hold their breath through these stretches — about 15 feet each, navigable in one Athletics DC 14 swim check or two short breath-holds.

The hidden grate

At the far east end of the slit, past the Black Ledger Gate's foundation, the slit narrows and ends in an iron grate. Perception DC 15 spots that the grate has been tampered with recently — a hinge has been worked loose, possibly to allow something (or someone) to pass through it. Investigation DC 14 confirms: the tampering is fresh, within the last few days. The route's own people may have prepared this as a quiet escape route.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Athletics14Crosses or crawls the slit; navigates an air-pocket section.
Athletics15Climbs out of the slit at a non-bridge point.
Survival13Identifies air movement and water current direction; estimates where the slit goes.
Perception15Hears route signals through side ducts; finds the tampered grate at the east end.
Investigation14Confirms recent tampering with the east-end grate.
Constitution13Endures cold/exertion on long swims.
Dexterity save12–14Avoids water pulse effects.

Location 11 — Under-Road Marker Gallery

A long, low corridor running from the back of the Account Hall to the Warden Post. About 60 feet long, 8 feet wide, 7 feet tall. The walls of this corridor are covered in route marks — not the tally registry of the Account Hall, but a different system: wayfinding marks for the route beyond this waystation.

What the party sees

The gallery is dim. Two lamps at each end. Between them, the walls are dense with marks — small cuts, arcs, dots, and symbols, sometimes overlapping, sometimes in neat horizontal bands, sometimes scattered. Some are very old; the cuts are softened by centuries. Some are fresh, with chips still on the floor below them.

Surface language is gone here. No words. No numerals. No abbreviations of any surface tongue you have ever seen. Only marks.

One mark is everywhere — the seven-step symbol you have seen at the Tarnsmere cairn and the eighth landing's tally. It appears on its own, in groups, with annotations, with cancellations. It is the route's identifier for this route — the Tarnsmere descent.

Another mark appears with about half the frequency: the black hill symbol you have known since the cairn. It indicates a sealed-entrance convention.

And there are others. Unfamiliar route cuts. Erased marks where someone has scraped the wall clean — sometimes recently, sometimes long ago. A fresh mark high on the wall: a narrow vertical stroke crossed at three-quarter height — Kethren's courier mark, made within the last week, indicating his recent passage.

And, at the far end of the gallery, on the lintel above the Warden Post's door, a single mark you have seen only once before. A shallow arc with three small notches at its base.

The deeper-authority mark

The mark over the Warden Post lintel is the same mark the party saw at the top of certain entries on the Witness Registry Wall — the deeper-authority mark. Here, it is incorporated into the architecture, not just into a registry entry. This authority's permission is woven into the waystation itself. The waystation was, at some point, formally authorised by whoever this mark belongs to. The route does not write their name.

(GM note: this is “E.’s” mark in its full ritual form. The party can record it. They cannot resolve it without later evidence.)

What the party can identify

  • The seven-step symbol — identified easily; confirmed as the Tarnsmere route mark.
  • The black hill — identified easily; confirmed as a sealed-entrance convention.
  • Kethren's mark, fresh — identified easily by anyone who recognises his courier signature.
  • Erased marks — History DC 15 estimates how old the erasures are; Investigation DC 16 can recover partial traces. The route has been editing its own wayfinding here.
  • The unfamiliar route cuts — History DC 16 confirms that no known surface polity uses these cuts. They are not Aerdy, not Suel-derived, not from any of the Cairn Hills mining traditions. The route does not derive from surface civilisations.
  • The deeper-authority mark — History DC 16 fails to identify it from any surface record. Arcana DC 16 detects old, dormant warding bound to the mark — not a spell, but the mark has been maintained as a procedural symbol.

The branch indicators

Toward the far end of the gallery, the marks become structured around a clear three-way branching motif. The motif is the route's way of saying: three routes diverge beyond this gate. The marks identifying each branch are:

  • Branch 1: a recovery sign, identifiable as the same mark used on the Haskur registry entry. This is the Haskur transfer route.
  • Branch 2: Kethren's courier mark, paired with the seven-cuts variant. This is the escort route Kethren uses.
  • Branch 3: a cargo-tally mark, paired with seed-and-wax indicators. This is the supply recovery route.

The party will see these same branch marks at the Lower Route Fork (Location 14) after the Black Ledger Gate.

Important: do not explain the E. mark

The deeper-authority mark above the Warden Post lintel should remain unexplained. The party can collect it (wax impression, sketch, rubbing). They can speculate. They should not be told it is “E.’s” mark, or that “E.” is anyone in particular. Later evidence in Phase 10 will let them connect it to the surface “E.” marginalia from Phase 4 and the Phase 6 evidence. For now: a pattern they can recognise but not name.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation15Distinguishes fresh from old marks; identifies Kethren's recent passage.
History16Confirms that no known surface polity owns the oldest marks; identifies the branch motif.
Arcana16Detects old, dormant warding on the deeper-authority mark; confirms the warding is procedural, not active.
Religion17Notes that none of the marks are religious in origin. (Useful negative information.)

Handout

The party recovers Handout 8 — Lower Route Marks here. See the Handouts journal.

Location 12 — Warden Post

A small chamber, about 12 feet square, at the end of the Under-Road Marker Gallery. The Warden Post is the waystation's control room. It is the only chamber with a direct view (through a slit window) into the Account Hall, and the only chamber with mechanical access to the Black Ledger Gate.

What the party sees

The chamber is plain. A stone desk along the east wall, a small token box atop it, a signal cord descending from the ceiling and disappearing into a brass channel that runs along the wall toward the gate. A small brazier of burn oil sits in a niche, lit. A route bell hangs on an iron arm above the desk, ready to swing.

The slit window in the west wall looks out at chest height over the Account Hall, with a clear view of the seven counter-bays and the central floor. Anyone working in the hall can be seen from here without themselves seeing the warden.

On the desk: a sealed instruction strip, the route bell's striker, a wax stylus, three witness tokens, and a single recovery token.

What's in the chamber

The Warden Instruction Strip (Handout 6)

The sealed strip on the desk is the warden's current operating instructions, current to within the week. Reading it (Investigation DC 14, no skill needed if simply unfolded):

  • “Admit courier with seven-cut authority.” — Kethren is the named carrier.
  • “Hold recovered surface asset (Andren) until witnessed.” — Haskur's transit was pending a witness when the strip was written.
  • “Burn surface copy of Andren paperwork.” — the H.V.A. slip in the Burn Furnace.
  • “Do not preserve Andren's spoken name in any registry.” — Haskur is being scrubbed from surface association.
  • “If trespassers descend, correct category before removal.” — the warden's first action on intruders is to assign them a route category (witness, cargo, escort, recovered) rather than to expel or kill them. This is the route's standard practice.
  • “Defer all questions of higher authority to channel beyond Black Ledger Gate.” — the warden does not have the rank to discuss whoever the deeper authority is.

The signal cord and route bell

The signal cord runs from the ceiling through the brass channel to the Black Ledger Gate's mechanism. Pulling it sends a route signal: arrivals being processed, gate request, emergency, depending on the cadence of the pull. Thieves' Tools DC 15 or Sleight of Hand DC 15 disables the cord without anyone noticing — this prevents Savax (if present) from signaling the gate.

The route bell, struck, sounds in three places: the Account Hall, the Burn Furnace, and the Black Ledger Gate antechamber. Striking it advances the Alert Clock by 1.

The token box

The small box contains three witness tokens and one recovery token. The recovery token is significant: it was prepared for Haskur's witnessed transfer but never used — the witness who was supposed to attest to his transfer never arrived (or, depending on play state, was killed/recalled before doing so). The token is therefore declarable — the party could use it to declare a witnessed recovery at the Black Ledger Gate, claiming Haskur is their recovered asset.

This is risky but powerful: it routes the party through the gate as “escort plus one recovered asset.” If the GM decides Haskur is alive and with them, this is dramatically perfect. If Haskur is dead, the recovery token still works — the route does not check very hard whether the asset is alive, as long as the documentation is in order.

The burn oil

The small brazier of burn oil in the niche is the warden's self-destruction option. If the waystation is fully compromised, the warden uses this burn oil to ignite the Account Hall counter-bays. The party can disable this by smothering the brazier (Dexterity save DC 12 to do so cleanly).

If Savax is here

This is Savax's working chamber. He is here in most of the opening scenarios. If the party approaches stealthily, they can:

  • Observe through the slit window (Stealth DC 14 to approach unseen).
  • Engage in conversation through the chamber door — Savax will speak through the door without opening it, in his procedural register.
  • Combat — Savax fights from this chamber's narrow doorway, using the slit window for visibility and the route bell for support.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Stealth14Approach unseen from the gallery.
Investigation14Find instructions, recovery token, token box contents.
Sleight of Hand / Thieves' Tools15Disable signal cord without detection.
Dexterity save12Smother brazier cleanly.

Handout

The party recovers Handout 6 — Warden Instruction Strip here.

Carry-forward

The recovery token from the Warden Post is one of the cleanest pathways through the Black Ledger Gate. The party should leave with it.
